title: Wail - solo adventures in a dream-like fantasy world (solo RPG)
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZzYTRvOWH1I

Daniel (@dungeondive) takes a look at Wail, a rules-light solo tabletop RPG by Mark Cook (bought physical from his Etsy; also on itch), plus the Mysteries of the Wailing Lands supplement. It's a half-hex-crawl, half-journaling game with a strong FromSoft flavour — Dark Souls / Demon's Souls / Elden Ring — in which you wake, lost and alone, in the morphing Wailing Lands, with the dilapidated Wey Castle as your home base (a Firelink-Shrine-like hub where rescued NPCs set up shop).

The engine is a simple d6 dice pool (roll one die, add or remove dice per relevant keyword, take the highest, fives/sixes succeed), tested only when something dangerous is at stake. There are no stats — characters are built by answering history questions for keywords and items — and the main threat is accumulating injuries until death. You explore hex by hex, rolling regions, points of interest, encounters and world-building tables, building the map as you go.

He demonstrates richly: a lake with two undying bridge-warriors locked in perpetual battle, a den of Jolly Robin-Hood thieves at Colony Bay, and a plot-starter quest to stop witches cursing an elder. He praises its balance of journaling and structured hex-crawl, while noting it leans on the player to supply challenges (and lacks dungeon generators — he'd import one).

----

Does Wail's blend of FromSoft-flavoured hex-crawl and journaling appeal — and how much player-supplied invention is too much for you?
